name: Sustainable Building Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most tightly focused of the Hillier certificates: five 3-credit courses form a coherent high-performance-building sequence, from net-zero fundamentals (ARCH 621) to quantitative energy simulation (ARCH 623 Building Energy Modeling with EnergyPlus), rigorous certification standards (ARCH 625 Passive House and Beyond), and responsive/adaptive systems (ARCH 626 Building Dynamics), with ARCH 684 Topics of Sustainable Urbanism providing city-scale context. The set builds logically from concepts to tools to standards.
dimensions.suggestion: Signal a suggested order (e.g., ARCH 621 before the ARCH 623 modeling course) so students see the concept-to-simulation progression even within a flat menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 with missingCourseCount 0 and no dangling references, which is appropriate for standalone graduate certificate electives. The only latent concern is that ARCH 623's EnergyPlus-based simulation realistically assumes building-science fundamentals yet lists no prerequisite, so an unprepared student could enter the modeling course cold.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a soft prerequisite or preparatory note on ARCH 623 recommending ARCH 621 or equivalent building-science background before the energy-modeling work.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the specific competencies (whole-building energy modeling, Passive House detailing) the certificate certifies are not stated for prospective students or employers.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the measurable sustainable-design skills (e.g., produce a calibrated EnergyPlus model, detail a Passive House envelope) a completer gains.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and there are no requirement groups, so although five 3-credit courses imply a focused ~15-credit (or choose-4/12-credit) credential, the total and the required-versus-elective count are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is null and treated as a graduate transparency note, not a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and whether all five courses are required or a subset is chosen.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are unusually specific and current, naming concrete tools and standards (EnergyPlus whole-building simulation, Passive House, Living Building Challenge, Enterprise Green Communities), which orients a reader well; the entry loses points only for lacking a plan-of-study block and a stated credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief credential header with total credits, course-selection rules, and intended audience above the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The content maps directly onto current decarbonization practice: net-zero design, whole-building energy modeling, Passive House and regenerative-design certification pathways, and adaptive/responsive architecture informed by material science and embedded systems (ARCH 626). This is a genuinely up-to-date professional-development offering.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep currency by adding an embodied-carbon/life-cycle-assessment component so the certificate covers whole-life carbon, not only operational energy.

descriptionQuality.justification: All five descriptions are present, specific, and jargon-appropriate, citing named simulation software (EnergyPlus), named standards (Passive House, Living Building Challenge), and concrete research areas (biology, material science, embedded systems, robotics in ARCH 626); none is thin or placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: No dangling references exist (missingCourseCount 0) and all five courses cleanly carry no prerequisite, which is unambiguous; the only clarity gap is the absence of any recommended preparation for the technically demanding ARCH 623 energy-modeling course.

missingTopics.rationale: ARCH 621 and ARCH 623 address operational energy, but no course covers whole-life/embodied carbon accounting (EC3, LCA), now a core sustainable-design competency.
The certificate teaches modeling and design but not how to verify as-built performance against models through commissioning and post-occupancy M&V.

missingTopics.topic: Electrification, heat pumps, and grid-interactive buildings
missingTopics.rationale: Decarbonization increasingly hinges on all-electric systems and demand flexibility, which the current envelope/energy focus does not explicitly address.

missingTopics.topic: Daylighting, thermal comfort, and occupant health
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond energy, human-centered performance (daylight autonomy, comfort, WELL) is expected in contemporary sustainable design but is not represented.
Material selection for carbon reduction, especially mass timber, is a fast-growing design driver absent from the five listed courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: The field is shifting from operational-only to embodied-plus-operational carbon; extending ARCH 621/623 with LCA tools would keep the certificate at the frontier.

emergingTrends.trend: Grid-interactive efficient buildings and demand flexibility
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 626 Building Dynamics already engages responsive systems, a natural bridge to building-to-grid controls and load flexibility.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML-driven energy optimization and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 623's EnergyPlus workflow can be modernized with machine-learning surrogate models and calibrated digital twins now common in practice.
ARCH 625 already references the Living Building Challenge; formalizing circularity and material reuse would deepen the regenerative-design thread.
Passive survivability, overheating risk, and flood-resilient detailing are rising priorities that complement the certificate's efficiency focus.

topSuggestions: Add an embodied-carbon / life-cycle-assessment course so the certificate covers whole-life carbon rather than operational energy alone.
Publish the total credits and course-selection rules (all five required vs. choose a subset) to fix the null credit transparency.
Publish 3-5 measurable learning outcomes (e.g., build a calibrated EnergyPlus model, detail a Passive House envelope).
Add a recommended-preparation note to the technically demanding ARCH 623 energy-modeling course.
Introduce measurement & verification / commissioning content so students can validate as-built performance against their models.
